is a [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") station complex on the [IND Fulton Street](/wiki/IND_Fulton_Street_Line "IND Fulton Street Line"), [IND Culver](/wiki/IND_Culver_Line "IND Culver Line"), and [BMT Fourth Avenue](/wiki/BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line "BMT Fourth Avenue Line") lines. The complex is located in the vicinity of [MetroTech Center](/wiki/MetroTech_Center "MetroTech Center") (near Jay and Willoughby Streets) in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"). It is served by the [A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), and [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") trains at all times; the [C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)") train at all times except late nights; the [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)") train during late nights only; and a few rush-hour [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains in the peak direction and [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)") trains in the reverse peak direction.

was built by the [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System") (IND) in 1933, while the **Lawrence Street–MetroTech station** was built by the [Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation") (BMT) in 1924. Despite being one block away from each other, the two stations were not connected for 77 years. As part of a station renovation completed in 2010, the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") (MTA) built a passageway to connect the two stations and made the complex fully compliant with the [Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990](/wiki/Americans_with_Disabilities_Act_of_1990 "Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990"). Both stations also contain "money train" platforms, which were formerly used to deliver MTA token revenue to neighboring [370 Jay Street](/wiki/370_Jay_Street "370 Jay Street").

The **Jay Street–MetroTech station** is a [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") station complex on the [IND Fulton Street](/wiki/IND_Fulton_Street_Line "IND Fulton Street Line"), [IND Culver](/wiki/IND_Culver_Line "IND Culver Line"), and [BMT Fourth Avenue](/wiki/BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line "BMT Fourth Avenue Line") lines. The complex is located in the vicinity of [MetroTech Center](/wiki/MetroTech_Center "MetroTech Center") (near Jay and Willoughby Streets) in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"). It is served by the [A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), and [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") trains at all times; the [C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)") train at all times except late nights; the [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)") train during late nights only; and a few rush-hour [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains in the peak direction and [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)") trains in the reverse peak direction.

The complex consists of two distinct, perpendicular stations. The **Jay Street–Borough Hall station** was built by the [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System") (IND) in 1933, while the **Lawrence Street–MetroTech station** was built by the [Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation") (BMT) in 1924. Despite being one block away from each other, the two stations were not connected for 77 years. As part of a station renovation completed in 2010, the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") (MTA) built a passageway to connect the two stations and made the complex fully compliant with the [Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990](/wiki/Americans_with_Disabilities_Act_of_1990 "Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990"). Both stations also contain "money train" platforms, which were formerly used to deliver MTA token revenue to neighboring [370 Jay Street](/wiki/370_Jay_Street "370 Jay Street").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_station&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### BMT station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_station&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: BMT station")]

The [Dual Contracts](/wiki/Dual_Contracts "Dual Contracts") were formalized in March 1913, specifying new lines or expansions to be built by the [Interborough Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Interborough_Rapid_Transit_Company "Interborough Rapid Transit Company") (IRT) and the [Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Brooklyn_Rapid_Transit_Company "Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company") (BRT; later the [Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation"), or BMT). The Dual Contracts included the construction of the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel"), which connected the [Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") with the [Fourth Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line "BMT Fourth Avenue Line") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") at [DeKalb Avenue station](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_station_(BMT_lines) "DeKalb Avenue station (BMT lines)").[[5]](#cite_note-nyt19130319-5) Originally, the only station on the Montague Street Tunnel in Brooklyn was to have been at [Court Street](/wiki/Court_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "Court Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)").[[6]](#cite_note-p575088610-6) After the contract was approved for the Montague Street Tunnel and the associated subway line, the planners realized there should have been a station at Lawrence Street.[[7]](#cite_note-The_Daily_Standard_Union_1916-7) In 1916, local business owners proposed an additional station at Lawrence and Willoughby Streets.[[7]](#cite_note-The_Daily_Standard_Union_1916-7)[[8]](#cite_note-n125408242-8) Supporters of the plan said the distance from the south end of the [Court Street station](/wiki/Court_Street_station_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "Court Street station (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") to the north end of the DeKalb Avenue station was 3,200 feet (980 m) apart, much longer than comparable stations on the IRT and BRT in Lower Manhattan and Downtown Brooklyn.[[8]](#cite_note-n125408242-8) The original contract was modified in July 1917, and a provision for the station was added.[[9]](#cite_note-era-aug-2010-9)

On May 16, 1918, the [New York Public Service Commission](/wiki/New_York_Public_Service_Commission "New York Public Service Commission") approved a report by the Chief Engineer requesting that work on the construction of the station stop due to a wartime shortage of materials and men due to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). Only one-ninth of the labor estimated to be required to allow the construction of the station to be completed along with the rest of the line was available. With this reduced labor force, work on this station could not be completed before July 1919, and work on the Court Street station could not be finished before April 1919, following the completion of the Montague Street Tunnel. It was decided to postpone work to complete this station, and use the labor force working on this station and concrete material intended to be used at the station to complete work on the Court Street station, accelerating the estimated completion of that station to January 1919, allowing service through the tunnel to operate in early 1919 as opposed to late 1919.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Construction stopped on May 18, when about half the station was completed. Service running through the Montague Tunnel and this station began on August 1, 1920, with the station being constructed alongside in-service trains.[[9]](#cite_note-era-aug-2010-9) The line was called the Montague Street Tunnel Line.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Construction resumed on May 18, 1922. The scope of work included excavation from the street to provide an entrance, the construction of an island platform between the two cast iron-lined tunnels covered by a steel and concrete roof, and the construction of a passageway, mezzanine and entrances. On June 11, 1924, the Lawrence Street station opened[[9]](#cite_note-era-aug-2010-9) with the Lawrence Street entrances; the Bridge Street entrances opened later.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

On March 29, 1993, Lawrence Street was renamed Lawrence Street–MetroTech to celebrate the revival of Downtown Brooklyn with the opening of the MetroTech complex. In response to increased ridership at the station from traffic MetroTech generated, new directional signs were installed, a wall that blocked the view of the token booth clerk was removed to improve security, a part-time token booth was added, and lighting was upgraded.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### IND station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_station&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: IND station")]

New York City mayor [John Francis Hylan](/wiki/John_Francis_Hylan "John Francis Hylan")'s original plans for the [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System") (IND), proposed in 1922, included building over 100 miles (160 km) of new lines and taking over nearly 100 miles (160 km) of existing lines, which would compete with the IRT and BMT.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) On December 9, 1924, the [New York City Board of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_City_Board_of_Transportation "New York City Board of Transportation") (BOT) gave preliminary approval for the construction of the [IND Eighth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line "IND Eighth Avenue Line").[[16]](#cite_note-nyt19241210-16) This line consisted of a corridor connecting [Inwood, Manhattan](/wiki/Inwood,_Manhattan "Inwood, Manhattan"), to [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), running largely under [Eighth Avenue](/wiki/Eighth_Avenue_(Manhattan) "Eighth Avenue (Manhattan)") but also paralleling [Greenwich Avenue](/wiki/Greenwich_Avenue "Greenwich Avenue") and [Sixth Avenue](/wiki/Sixth_Avenue "Sixth Avenue") in Lower Manhattan.[[16]](#cite_note-nyt19241210-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Raskin-RoutesNotTaken-20132-17) An additional line, the IND Sixth Avenue Line, was approved in 1925, running from [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") underneath Sixth Avenue, [Houston Street](/wiki/Houston_Street "Houston Street"), [Essex Street](/wiki/Essex_Street "Essex Street"), and the [Rutgers Street Tunnel](/wiki/Rutgers_Street_Tunnel "Rutgers Street Tunnel") to Downtown Brooklyn.[[18]](#cite_note-18) By July 1927, the BOT had finalized its plans for new IND lines in Brooklyn. The Eighth Avenue Line was to continue into eastern Brooklyn as the [Fulton Street Line](/wiki/IND_Fulton_Street_Line "IND Fulton Street Line"), while the Sixth Avenue Line was to continue to [South Brooklyn](/wiki/South_Brooklyn "South Brooklyn") as the [Smith Street (later Culver) Line](/wiki/IND_Culver_Line "IND Culver Line"). The lines were to intersect under Jay Street in Downtown Brooklyn.[[19]](#cite_note-p1113741083-19)

The Jay Street–Borough Hall station was part of a three-stop extension of the IND Eighth Avenue Line from [Chambers Street](/wiki/Chambers_Street_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "Chambers Street (IND Eighth Avenue Line)") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan").[[20]](#cite_note-Jay2-20)[[21]](#cite_note-BklynEagle-INDJaySt-HighSt-Jan1933-21)[[22]](#cite_note-BklyEagle-SandHogs-INDJayFulton-1930-22) Construction of the extension began in June 1928.[[22]](#cite_note-BklyEagle-SandHogs-INDJayFulton-1930-22) The extension opened to Jay Street on February 1, 1933.[[20]](#cite_note-Jay2-20)[[23]](#cite_note-NYTimes-IND8AvJaySt-Jan1933-23) The outer tracks first saw service on March 20, 1933, when the IND Culver Line opened.[[17]](#cite_note-Raskin-RoutesNotTaken-20132-17)[[24]](#cite_note-BklynEagle-INDBergen-Mar121933-24)[[25]](#cite_note-NYTimes-INDBergen-Mar121933-25) The IND Sixth Avenue Line to [West Fourth Street–Washington Square](/wiki/West_Fourth_Street%E2%80%93Washington_Square_(New_York_City_Subway) "West Fourth Street–Washington Square (New York City Subway)") opened on April 9, 1936,[[26]](#cite_note-26) and the Fulton Street Line to [Rockaway Avenue](/wiki/Rockaway_Avenue_(IND_Fulton_Street_Line) "Rockaway Avenue (IND Fulton Street Line)") opened the same day.[[27]](#cite_note-nyt19360409-27)

Until 1969, a free transfer was available to/from the [BMT Myrtle Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Myrtle_Avenue_Line "BMT Myrtle Avenue Line") at [Bridge–Jay Streets](/wiki/Bridge%E2%80%93Jay_Streets_(BMT_Myrtle_Avenue_Line) "Bridge–Jay Streets (BMT Myrtle Avenue Line)") and also issued at stations from [Sumner Avenue](/wiki/Sumner_Avenue_(BMT_Myrtle_Avenue_Line) "Sumner Avenue (BMT Myrtle Avenue Line)") on south. When the Myrtle Avenue Line south of [Myrtle Avenue](/wiki/Myrtle_Avenue_(BMT_Jamaica_Line) "Myrtle Avenue (BMT Jamaica Line)") closed, the transfer was issued to the B54 bus, which ran along the former route.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Today, the [MetroCard](/wiki/MetroCard_(New_York_City) "MetroCard (New York City)") provides free transfer between bus and subway throughout the system.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

#### Experimental installations and programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_station&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Experimental installations and programs")]

In 1955, the city decided to experiment with placing raised safety disks on the edges of the platforms, in order to increase passenger safety. Compared to the painted orange-and-yellow stripes on the platforms, the disks, which were painted yellow and spaced one foot apart from each other, were expected to last about five times as long. The northbound platform's disks were 4 inches (10 cm) in diameter, and the southbound platform's were 3 inches (7.6 cm).[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 1957, the city conducted another experiment, this time placing an automatic token dispenser in the station.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In September 1987, the station was the site of yet another experiment; the station's turnstiles were converted to allow new fare payment, consisting of "laminated polyester fare cards."[[32]](#cite_note-32) (This would later become the MetroCard, which was not widely released until 1993.)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The station's token booths were shuttered in May 2005, after fare tokens were replaced with [MetroCards](/wiki/MetroCard "MetroCard"); station agents were deployed elsewhere in the station to answer passengers' queries. This was part of a pilot program that was tested at seven other stations.[[34]](#cite_note-nyt-2005-05-03-34)

In October 2019, the MTA unveiled an accessible station lab at Jay Street–MetroTech station, which was to run until the end of the year. The lab includes over a dozen features including Braille signs, tactile pads, wayfinding apps, diagrams of accessible routes, and floor stickers to guide passengers to the correct routes.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Complex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_station&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Complex")]

In 1981, the [MTA](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") had listed the IND portion of the station among the 69 most deteriorated stations in the subway system.[[39]](#cite_note-39) However, in 2005, planned renovation of twelve subway stations, including the Jay Street and Lawrence Street stations, was delayed indefinitely.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The stations were separate from each other since the IND station's opening, despite their proximity. In March 2007, a contract was finally awarded for the renovation of the stations.[[2]](#cite_note-:5-2) The MTA constructed a 175-foot (53 m) transfer passageway as part of its 2005–2009 Capital Program.[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41) The $164.5 million project also brought the stations into compliance with the [Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990](/wiki/Americans_with_Disabilities_Act_of_1990 "Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990")[[2]](#cite_note-:5-2)[[42]](#cite_note-42) and cosmetically improved the upper mezzanine.[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41) With the opening of the transfer on December 10, 2010, the complex was given its present name.[[2]](#cite_note-:5-2)[[3]](#cite_note-unveils-3)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) The transfer was projected to benefit an estimated 35,000 daily passengers.[[2]](#cite_note-:5-2)

In 2016, a new entrance to the BMT portion of the station was built as part of the [AVA DoBro](/wiki/AVA_DoBro "AVA DoBro") residential high-rise building. This entrance replaces an earlier entrance at the southeast corner of Willoughby and Bridge Streets, the corner where the building is located.[[45]](#cite_note-stopstair-45) The MTA was hopeful that this instance would encourage developers to build other entrances to other subway stations, since AVA DoBro's developer paid for the entrance in its entirety.[[46]](#cite_note-News_12_Brooklyn_2016-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) New York City councilmember [Lincoln Restler](/wiki/Lincoln_Restler "Lincoln Restler") founded a volunteer group, the Friends of MTA Station Group, in early 2023 to advocate for improvements to the Jay Street–MetroTech station and four other subway stations in Brooklyn.[[48]](#cite_note-Brendlen_2023-48)[[49]](#cite_note-Nessen_2023-49) The MTA announced in 2025 that a customer service center would open at the station.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Connecting passageway between the stations

The station consists of three underground levels. Just below ground is the IND mezzanine, then the IND platforms, followed by the BMT platform on the deepest level.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)[[52]](#cite_note-:3-52) The two stations connect to each other via a stair, two escalators, and an elevator at the west end of the BMT station. The BMT station also has its own mezzanine at its eastern end.[[2]](#cite_note-:5-2) The stations are located one block away from each other.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

The 2009 artwork in this station is called *Departures and Arrivals* by Ben Snead. It consists of a 173-foot (53 m) long glass mosaic depicting animals including starlings, sparrows, lion fish, parrots, tiger beetles, and koi fish.[[54]](#cite_note-54) It was installed as part of the [MTA Arts for Transit](/wiki/MTA_Arts_%26_Design "MTA Arts & Design") program during the station complex's renovation.[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41)

### Entrances and exits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_station&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Entrances and exits")]

The full-time IND/BMT entrance is at the center and has a [turnstile](/wiki/Turnstile "Turnstile") bank, token booth, and a single street stair leading to the northeast corner of Willoughby and Jay Streets, while a set of staircases and escalators and one [ADA-accessible](/wiki/Americans_with_Disabilities_Act_of_1990 "Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990") elevator lead to the northwest corner underneath [370 Jay Street](/wiki/370_Jay_Street "370 Jay Street"), the former headquarters of the [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System").[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)[[55]](#cite_note-MTA-NameofNeighborhood-2015-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Jay_St_BMT_new_entrance_vc.jpg/250px-Jay_St_BMT_new_entrance_vc.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay_St_BMT_new_entrance_vc.jpg)

Entrance to BMT platform at southeast corner of Bridge and Willoughby Streets, built in 2016

The other two entrances/exits are unstaffed. The one at the north end has a weekday-only turnstile bank and token booth, [full height turnstiles](/wiki/HEET_turnstile "HEET turnstile"), and a wide staircase to [MetroTech Center](/wiki/MetroTech_Center "MetroTech Center") and another stair and four escalators to the former [New York City Transit](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit "New York City Transit") Headquarters,[[56]](#cite_note-:6-56) a mostly vacant 13-story building at 370 Jay Street.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)[[55]](#cite_note-MTA-NameofNeighborhood-2015-55) These escalators were installed as part of a 1952 improvement, as were the squarish "Subway" entrance lamps that are found only in a few other places in the system.[[57]](#cite_note-57) These were designed in [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco")/[Art Moderne](/wiki/Streamline_Moderne "Streamline Moderne") style.[[58]](#cite_note-BklynArchGuide-58) The building itself has a memorial to New York City Transit workers who died in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[56]](#cite_note-:6-56) The entrance/exit at the south end has only full height turnstiles and two staircases leading to either side of Jay and Fulton Streets.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)[[55]](#cite_note-MTA-NameofNeighborhood-2015-55)

The full-time BMT-only entrance is at Lawrence and Willoughby Streets near the west end. It has two platform stairs facing the opposite direction, a small turnstile bank, token booth, and four stairs to the two eastern corners of the aforementioned intersection. The stairs serve the BMT platform directly.[[52]](#cite_note-:3-52)[[55]](#cite_note-MTA-NameofNeighborhood-2015-55)

There is an additional [full-height turnstile](/wiki/HEET_turnstile "HEET turnstile") entrance at the east end. It formerly contained a booth and has two street stairs to Bridge and Willoughby Streets, high turnstiles, and two platform stairs. This fare control area was the first in the system to have its service gate converted to an emergency exit. An exit-only escalator on the BMT platform also leads to the southeast corner's entrance/exit.[[52]](#cite_note-:3-52)[[55]](#cite_note-MTA-NameofNeighborhood-2015-55) The AVA DoBro building contains stairs and an elevator, which connect to the eastern, full-height turnstile entrance. Unlike the elevator entrance at Jay and Willoughby Streets, this elevator entrance is not ADA-accessible.[[46]](#cite_note-News_12_Brooklyn_2016-46)

The station has a total of 16 staircase/escalator entrances and 2 elevator entrances.[[55]](#cite_note-MTA-NameofNeighborhood-2015-55) Full-time entrances are indicated in green, and part-time entrances are indicated in red.

The **Jay Street–MetroTech station** (formerly **Jay Street–Borough Hall station** before the construction of the station complex) is an express station on both the IND Fulton Street and Culver lines. It has four tracks with two [island platforms](/wiki/Island_platform "Island platform"). The station is served by the [A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)") and [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)") at all times and by the [C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)") except at night. Fulton Street Line trains (the A and C) use the center "express" tracks, while Culver Line trains (the F) use the outer "local" tracks.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51) Current service patterns route all [IND Eighth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line "IND Eighth Avenue Line") trains to the Fulton Street Line and all [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") trains to the Culver Line. As such, for A and C trains, the station is between [High Street](/wiki/High_Street_station_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "High Street station (IND Eighth Avenue Line)") to the north and [Hoyt–Schermerhorn Streets](/wiki/Hoyt%E2%80%93Schermerhorn_Streets_station "Hoyt–Schermerhorn Streets station") to the south. For F trains, the station is between [York Street](/wiki/York_Street_station_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "York Street station (IND Sixth Avenue Line)") to the north and [Bergen Street](/wiki/Bergen_Street_station_(IND_Culver_Line) "Bergen Street station (IND Culver Line)") to the south.[[59]](#cite_note-submap-59) [Diamond crossovers](/wiki/Diamond_crossover "Diamond crossover") north of the station permit Eighth Avenue–Culver or Sixth Avenue–Fulton Street service; these switches are only used during service disruptions.[[60]](#cite_note-:2-60)

The station originally measured 600 feet (180 m) long, and each platform measures about 30 feet (9.1 m) wide.[[61]](#cite_note-p1114755744-61) The station has blue [I-beam](/wiki/I-beam "I-beam") columns on the Manhattan-bound platform and white concrete tile columns on the Brooklyn-bound one.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51) The station's walls had blue tiles.[[61]](#cite_note-p1114755744-61) Before renovation, the trim line on the platform walls was two-tone cobalt blue with "JAY" tiled in white lettering on a black background underneath.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51) As part of the renovation, new tiling was placed on the trackside walls. After the renovation, the blue trim-line was widened and a double border of Heather Blue and black was added. The new blue tile in the centre of the trim-line is also somewhat darker than the original, the new color being shown as "Midnight Blue".[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)

Each platform has six staircases and one elevator leading up to the full-length [mezzanine](/wiki/Mezzanine_(architecture) "Mezzanine (architecture)"). Before renovation, the entire mezzanine was inside fare control, but the mezzanine was split into two separate parts during the renovation.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51) Now, the mezzanine has a larger southern section connecting to the southern exits, the central exits, and the transfer to the BMT platform; as well as a smaller northern section connecting to the northern exits only. The two parts of the mezzanine are cut off by a large white wall.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)

The **Jay Street–MetroTech station** (formerly **Lawrence Street–MetroTech station** before the construction of the station complex) on the [BMT Fourth Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line "BMT Fourth Avenue Line") is a local station with two tracks and one narrow island platform.[[60]](#cite_note-:2-60) The station is served by the [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") at all times, by limited [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains during rush hours, and by the [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)") only during the night. The station is between [Court Street](/wiki/Borough_Hall/Court_Street_station#BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line_platform "Borough Hall/Court Street station") to the north and [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_station_(BMT_lines) "DeKalb Avenue station (BMT lines)") to the south.[[59]](#cite_note-submap-59) Unlike in the IND station, there are no tiles on the track walls.[[52]](#cite_note-:3-52)

A narrow mezzanine above the platform connects the station's two easternmost [fare control](/wiki/Fare_control "Fare control") areas. It still has its original directional signs labeled as "to Lawrence Street" and "to Bridge Street".[[52]](#cite_note-:3-52)

The platform formerly had a narrow up-only escalator that bypassed the Lawrence and Willoughby Streets fare control, and led to a small landing with two high exit-only gates. A short staircase then connected to the landing of the southeast street stairs to that intersection.[[52]](#cite_note-:3-52)

## Money train platforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_station&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Money train platforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Jay_money_train_door_jeh.JPG/250px-Jay_money_train_door_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jay_money_train_door_jeh.JPG)

[Money train](/wiki/Money_train "Money train") door on southbound track of the IND platform

Formerly, "[money trains](/wiki/Money_train "Money train")" collected the tokens that were used to pay fares at each of the subway stations and deposited them into a special door that led to a money-counting room under 370 Jay Street. The platforms were built in 1951,[[62]](#cite_note-Young_2016-62) the same year the building opened,[[63]](#cite_note-NYTimes-BOT-NewHome-Mar1951-63)[[64]](#cite_note-NYTimes-BOT-Rush-NewHome-Mar1951-64)[[65]](#cite_note-NYTimes-BOT-NewBuilding-Ap1951-65) though "money trains" had been in use on the system since 1905.[[66]](#cite_note-:4-66) The platforms were placed next to 370 Jay Street because it was a convenient location near where all three subway companies had tunnels.[[62]](#cite_note-Young_2016-62) Tokens became [New York City Transit fare media](/wiki/New_York_City_transit_fares "New York City transit fares") in 1951. Tokens were last used in the entire New York City Transit system, including the subway, in 2003. This meant that the money trains were no longer used, and in December 2006, the platforms were closed.[[62]](#cite_note-Young_2016-62)[[66]](#cite_note-:4-66) The money trains were also retired, though for a different reason: they moved slowly, and the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") was concerned that the money trains would delay train traffic.[[67]](#cite_note-am_New_York_2016-67) The money train later became part of the collection of the nearby [New York Transit Museum](/wiki/New_York_Transit_Museum "New York Transit Museum"), and in October 2015, the museum started hosting another exhibit, *The Secret Life of 370 Jay Street*, that chronicled the building's varying uses.[[68]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Daily_Eagle_2015-68)

Each of the three former companies that made up the current New York City Subway (the Independent Subway System, Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Company, and [Interborough Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Interborough_Rapid_Transit_Company "Interborough Rapid Transit Company")) had their own money train platforms.[[62]](#cite_note-Young_2016-62) IND money trains made their deposits from the southbound IND Culver line track,[[69]](#cite_note-Untapped_Cities_2016-69) and the still-visible door on the wall is where they connected to the vaults above before [armored trucks](/wiki/Armored_car_(valuables) "Armored car (valuables)") replaced them.[[62]](#cite_note-Young_2016-62) For the BMT, there was a second platform just west of the station, after a [diamond crossover](/wiki/Diamond_crossover "Diamond crossover") between the two tracks;[[70]](#cite_note-70) this was the deepest of the three money train platforms.[[69]](#cite_note-Untapped_Cities_2016-69) A third platform is also in the [IRT Eastern Parkway Line](/wiki/IRT_Eastern_Parkway_Line "IRT Eastern Parkway Line") tunnel that passes through this area for the same purpose.[[69]](#cite_note-Untapped_Cities_2016-69)
